title: Support nullable properties in ValidationMessageStore.Add expression-based overloads
author: BharatRamsf3693
body: ## Background and Motivation This proposal addresses the nullable-reference-type warning reported in: #41338 `ValidationMessageStore` provides overloads that allow validation messages to be associated with a field using an expression-based accessor. Today, these overloads accept `Expression>`, which causes nullable-reference-type warnings when the accessor targets a nullable property such as `string?`. ```csharp messages.Add(() => model.Text, "This value is not valid"); ``` When Text is declared as string?, the expression is converted to `Expression>`, requiring a conversion from a nullable reference type to object. Although the expression is only used to identify the field through FieldIdentifier.Create, the conversion can result in compiler nullability warnings and creates an unnecessary friction point for developers working in nullable-enabled projects. This proposal changes the expression-based overloads to use a generic type parameter so that the original field type, including its nullability annotations, is preserved. This allows nullable properties to be used naturally without warnings while maintaining the existing runtime behavior. Benefits include: - Eliminates compiler nullability warnings when using nullable properties. - Improves the developer experience for nullable-enabled applications. ## Proposed API ```diff namespace Microsoft.AspNetCore.Components.Forms; public sealed class ValidationMessageStore { - public void Add(Expression> accessor, string message) + public void Add(Expression> accessor, string message) - public void Add(Expression> accessor, IEnumerable messages) + public void Add(Expression> accessor, IEnumerable messages) } ``` ## Usage Examples Before: After: With the proposed API, TField is inferred as string?, eliminating nullable conversion warnings. ## Alternative Designs - The existing `Expression>` overloads could be retained and users could suppress or ignore nullable warnings. However, this leaves an avoidable warning in a common nullable-enabled scenario and provides a less polished developer experience. - Another option would be to add generic overloads while keeping the existing `Expression>` overloads. ```csharp public void Add(Expression> accessor, string message); public void Add(Expression> accessor, string message); ``` However, keeping both overloads would add unnecessary complexity. The generic version is more flexible and better preserves the actual type of the field, including its nullability. ## Risks The runtime behavior is expected to remain unchanged. The methods continue to use FieldIdentifier.Create to identify the target field. Overall, the change primarily affects compile-time type inference and nullable analysis, improving the developer experience without altering functionality.

title: Blazor WASM with fingerprinting enabled will make blazor.webassembly.{..}.js point to dotnet.js in error
author: ricardomomm
body:
### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [x] I have searched the existing issues

### Describe the bug

I'm using .Net 10 and I have enabled asset fingerprinting, I can see it is generating the files correctly when publishing but the content of blazor.webassembly.{fingerprint}.js is incorrectly referencing dotnet.js instead of dotnet.{fingerprint}.js and breaking the application.

```
load:function(e,t){return async function(e,t){const{dotnet:n}=await async function(e){if("undefined"==typeof WebAssembly||!WebAssembly.validate)throw new Error("This browser does not support WebAssembly.");if(e.loadBootResource){const t="dotnetjs",n=e.loadBootResource(t,"dotnet.js","_framework/dotnet.js","","js-module-dotnet");if("string"==typeof n){const e=new URL(n,document.baseURI).toString();return await import(e)}if(n)throw new Error(`For a ${t} resource, custom loaders must supply a URI string.`)}return await import("./dotnet.js")}
```

### Expected Behavior

I expect that when fingerprinting is enabled all references within the generated files would point to the fingerprinted files.

### Steps To Reproduce

* Create a new project
* Replace the blazor.webassembly.js with blazor.webassembly#[.{fingerprint}].js
* Add the following config to the csproj: <OverrideHtmlAssetPlaceholders>true</OverrideHtmlAssetPlaceholders>
* Publish

Try to run the application to see the error or just open the generated blazor.webassembly.{fingerprint}.js file and look for dotnet.js

### Exceptions (if any)

_No response_

### .NET Version

10.0.301

### Anything else?

Packages versions, tested with 10.0.5 and 10.0.9, same error

title: OutputCaching invalid cache keys can coalesce unrelated concurrent responses
author: rokonec
body:
## Summary

`OutputCacheMiddleware` can coalesce concurrent requests whose cache keys are invalid. `OutputCacheKeyProvider.CreateStorageKey` returns `string.Empty` when attacker-controlled vary input contains a reserved delimiter, such as an encoded record separator (`%1E`). The cache-lookup path correctly skips an empty key, but the response-locking path schedules work under that same empty string. Concurrent requests with otherwise unrelated cache identities can therefore share the first request's in-flight `OutputCacheEntry`.

Under the supplied evidence, this is a low-severity confidentiality vulnerability. Practical severity depends on whether an attacker can reliably overlap a targeted request and whether the affected application returns sensitive data on a default-policy-eligible unauthenticated response.

## Affected source flow

* `src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/OutputCacheKeyProvider.cs:40-60` returns `string.Empty` when any key component cannot be appended.
* `src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/OutputCacheKeyProvider.cs:203-233` and `:249-267` reject delimiter-containing query names or values. The default policy varies by all query parameters, so a value such as `%1E` reaches this path.
* `src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/OutputCacheMiddleware.cs:329-340` treats an empty key as invalid and skips persistent cache lookup.
* `src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/OutputCacheMiddleware.cs:133-155` creates the key for storage, then calls `_requestDispatcher.ScheduleAsync(context.CacheKey, ...)` whenever locking is allowed without checking whether the key is empty. A waiter subsequently calls `TryServeCachedResponseAsync` on the leader's in-flight entry.
* `src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/OutputCacheMiddleware.cs:419-443` refuses to store the completed entry when the key is empty.
* `src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/DispatcherExtensions.cs:7-47` groups all concurrent work by the supplied key. The empty string is non-null and is therefore a valid dispatcher key.
* `src/Middleware/OutputCaching/src/Policies/DefaultPolicy.cs:19-31` enables locking and varies by all query parameters. `:42-60` and `:63-88` limit default-policy caching to successful unauthenticated GET/HEAD responses without request authorization or response `Set-Cookie`.

## Concrete concurrent timeline

Assume two cache-enabled, default-policy-eligible endpoints produce distinguishable bodies and request A is held at the endpoint with a deterministic gate:

1. Request A sends `GET /a?x=%1E`. Key construction rejects the decoded delimiter and returns `""`. No cache entry is found. A enters `_requestDispatcher` as the leader for key `""` and begins generating body `A`.
2. While A is blocked, request B sends `GET /b?x=%1E`. Its otherwise different path also produces key `""`. B joins A's dispatcher work instead of executing `/b`.
3. Release A. Its successful response is represented by an in-flight `OutputCacheEntry` and returned to dispatcher waiters.
4. A completes normally. B receives A's status, headers, and body through `TryServeCachedResponseAsync`, even though B targeted `/b`.
5. The empty key prevents the entry from being written to `IOutputCacheStore`. A later non-overlapping request executes normally.

The same pattern can use two values or other key components that independently fail delimiter validation. Distinct paths make it clear that the collision is the invalid sentinel key, not an intended cache identity.

## Impact and constraints

The default policy materially constrains exposure: authenticated identities and requests carrying `Authorization` are excluded, and responses with `Set-Cookie`, non-200 status codes, or an authenticated user at response time are not stored. Applications can still return sensitive unauthenticated content based on implicit context such as tenant resolution, source network, custom headers, or application state, so cross-request response reuse should not occur for an invalid key.

This is not persistent cache poisoning. Empty keys are rejected by `TryServeFromCacheAsync` and `FinalizeCacheBodyAsync`, so the response is not stored. The disclosure exists only while requests overlap in `_requestDispatcher`; after the leader finishes, its dispatcher entry is removed.

## Proposed minimal fix

Require a valid key before using response locking:

```csharp
if (context.AllowLocking && !string.IsNullOrEmpty(context.CacheKey))
```

An invalid key should follow the existing unlocked path and execute the request normally. This matches the lookup and storage behavior, both of which already treat an empty key as a signal to skip cache operations.

Changing `WorkDispatcher` is less appropriate because an empty string can be a legitimate key for a generic non-null-key dispatcher; invalidity is specific to OutputCaching's cache-key contract. Changing `AllowCacheStorage` is also broader than necessary: policies requested storage, and the middleware already uses an empty generated key to suppress actual storage. Mutating that policy decision could affect response processing and custom policies while failing to express that only request coalescing is unsafe here.

## Deterministic regression test

Add a middleware test near the existing locking tests in `src/Middleware/OutputCaching/test/OutputCacheMiddlewareTests.cs`:

1. Configure the normal cache base policy with locking enabled.
2. Create request A for `/a?x=%1E` and request B for `/b?x=%1E`; verify separately that these inputs produce empty storage keys, consistent with the delimiter tests in `src/Middleware/OutputCaching/test/OutputCacheKeyProviderTests.cs:280-389`.
3. In the downstream delegate, use `TaskCompletionSource` gates rather than delays: signal when A enters, start B, require B to enter the delegate before releasing A, then release both.
4. Return distinct bodies (`A` and `B`) and count executions.
5. Assert that the delegate ran twice, A received `A`, B received `B`, and the store has no entry/set operation for either invalid key.

The test fails deterministically before the fix because B never enters the downstream delegate while A owns the empty-key dispatcher slot, and passes after locking is bypassed for the invalid key.

title: Support `IOptionsMonitor<SqlServerCacheOptions>` for runtime configuration updates
author: eskour
body:
## Description

`Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.SqlServer` currently relies on `IOptions<SqlServerCacheOptions>`, which effectively makes its configuration immutable after the service has been constructed.

This becomes a limitation in environments where the SQL Server connection string can change at runtime, such as:

- Secret rotation (Azure Key Vault, CyberArk, HashiCorp Vault, etc.)
- Custom `IConfigurationProvider`s that support reloads
- Dynamic multi-environment or failover scenarios

Although the ASP.NET Core configuration system supports runtime configuration reloads through `IOptionsMonitor<T>`, `SqlServerCache` does not take advantage of this capability. As a result, the cache continues using the original connection string until the application is restarted.

This forces applications to restart in order to pick up a new database connection string, even though the rest of the configuration pipeline is already reactive.

## Proposed solution

Replace the dependency on `IOptions<SqlServerCacheOptions>` with `IOptionsMonitor<SqlServerCacheOptions>`.

`SqlServerCache` could subscribe to configuration changes using `OnChange()` and recreate its internal database access components whenever the monitored options change.

A possible implementation would:

- Inject `IOptionsMonitor<SqlServerCacheOptions>`
- Initialize internal state from `CurrentValue`
- Subscribe to `OnChange()`
- Atomically replace the internal `DatabaseOperations` instance (for example via `Interlocked.Exchange`) whenever the options change
- Dispose the `OnChange()` registration when the cache is disposed

## Benefits

- Enable seamless connection string rotation without application restarts.
- Align `SqlServerCache` with the rest of the ASP.NET Core options ecosystem.
- Remain fully backward compatible for existing applications.
- Benefit users relying on configuration providers that support reload-on-change.
- Require only minimal implementation changes while preserving thread safety, since `DatabaseOperations` can be atomically replaced.

title: ResponseCaching: VaryByQueryKeys vary key can collide because the '=' name/value separator is not escaped
author: rokonec
body:
## Summary

`ResponseCachingKeyProvider.CreateStorageVaryByKey` builds the storage vary key by joining each name/value pair with a literal `=` character:

```csharp
builder.Append(KeyDelimiter)
    .AppendUpperInvariant(queryArray[i].Key)
    .Append('=')                 // literal '=' separator between key and value
    ...append values...
```

The `=` separator is a normal character that can legitimately appear inside a decoded query key or query value (and header value). Only the two control-char delimiters (`KeyDelimiter` `\x1e` and `KeySubDelimiter` `\x1f`) are guarded via `ThrowIfContainsDelimiters`; `=` is not. Because `HttpRequest.Query` exposes URL-decoded keys and values, a percent-encoded `%3D` decodes to a literal `=` that lands in the data, making the structural `=` ambiguous with the data.

As a result, two distinct sets of query inputs can serialize to the identical vary key and therefore share a single cache entry (a cache-key collision). This affects the wildcard `VaryByQueryKeys = ["*"]` path as well as the header and explicit query-key paths, since all three use the same unescaped `=` separator.

Example of two distinct inputs that produce the same key segment:

| Parsed key | Parsed value | Serialized segment |
|------------|--------------|--------------------|
| `a`        | `B=`         | `\x1eA=B=`         |
| `a=B`      | (empty)      | `\x1eA=B=`         |

## Affected code

`src/Middleware/ResponseCaching/src/ResponseCachingKeyProvider.cs`

- Header segment separator: [`.Append('=')`](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/src/Middleware/ResponseCaching/src/ResponseCachingKeyProvider.cs)
- Wildcard query segment separator: `.Append('=')`
- Explicit query-key segment separator: `.Append('=')`

## Proposed fix

Stop using the literal `=` as a structural separator between a name and its value, since `=` can appear in the (decoded) data. The data is already guaranteed to be free of the control-char delimiters by `ThrowIfContainsDelimiters`, so the cheapest robust fix is to **use a reserved control-character delimiter for the name/value separator** instead of `=`:

1. Introduce a dedicated separator constant (e.g. a group-separator control char `\x1d`) for the name↔value boundary.
2. Replace the three `.Append('=')` calls (header, wildcard query, explicit query) with that separator.
3. Extend `ThrowIfContainsDelimiters` to also reject the new separator character, keeping the invariant that structural characters never appear in keys or values.

This makes the serialization injective (distinct logical inputs always produce distinct keys), adds no per-character escaping/allocation on the hot path, and is consistent with the existing delimiter design. Note this changes the cache-key format, so existing cached entries are invalidated on upgrade, which is acceptable for a correctness fix.

Alternatively, each name/value could be escaped (percent-encode `=` and the delimiters) or length-prefixed; the control-char separator approach is preferred as the smallest, allocation-free change.

A unit test should be added asserting that inputs which differ only by the placement of an encoded `=` at the key/value boundary produce different vary keys.

title: Harden wildcard matching with empty segments inside
author: cincuranet
body: Both #67265 and #66582 are good direction, but we should also handle other empty segments - i.e. `foo..example.com` or `foo..bar.example.com`.

title: Connection downgrades to negotiateVersion=0 when hub URL contains the substring negotiateVersion
author: cincuranet
body:
When a hub URL's query string contains the substring `negotiateVersion` in an unrelated place (i.e. `/hub?x=negotiateVersion`), the client fails to append `negotiateVersion=1` to the negotiate request, and the connection silently negotiates at version 0 instead of 1. In version 0 the public `connectionId` is also used as the HTTP transport token.

We should parse `negotiateVersion` from the query string with an exact key match instead of a substring/contains check, so unrelated parameter names or values can't suppress it. We could also make version 0 explicit opt-in or warning on downgrade.

title: Make GetValidationAttributes virtual instead of abstract
author: Youssef1313

title: [dotnet-sdk-11.0.100-preview.7.26360.111] grandnode app launch failed with MethodNotFoundException
author: WeiweiCaiAcpt
body: ### Description When running against with dotnet-sdk-11.0.100-preview.7.26360.111, we found that the grandnode application failed to launch and displayed a blank web page. Investigation showed that the failure was caused by the following MethodNotFoundException: ``` Method not found: 'Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.IMvcBuilder Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.MvcCoreMvcBuilderExtensions.SetCompatibilityVersion(Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.IMvcBuilder, Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.CompatibilityVersion)'. ``` Based on the call stack, the exception originates from the Wkhtmltopdf.NetCore dependency during service registration: ``` at Wkhtmltopdf.NetCore.WkhtmltopdfConfiguration.AddWkhtmltopdf(IServiceCollection services, String wkhtmltopdfRelativePath) at Grand.Framework.Infrastructure.Extensions.ServiceCollectionExtensions.ConfigureApplicationServices(IServiceCollection services, IConfiguration configuration) in F:\AppSources\Grand.Framework\Infrastructure\Extensions\ServiceCollectionExtensions.cs:line 64 at Grand.Web.Startup.ConfigureServices(IServiceCollection services) in F:\AppSources\Grand.Web\Startup.cs:line 50 at System.Reflection.MethodBaseInvoker.InvokeWithOneArg(Object obj, BindingFlags invokeAttr, Binder binder, Object[] parameters, CultureInfo culture) at System.Reflection.RuntimeMethodInfo.Invoke(Object obj, BindingFlags invokeAttr, Binder binder, Object[] parameters, CultureInfo culture) at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.ConfigureServicesBuilder.InvokeCore(Object instance, IServiceCollection services) at Microsoft.AspNetCore.Hosting.GenericWebHostBuilder.UseStartup(Type startupType, HostBuilderContext context, IServiceCollection services, Object instance) ``` ### Reproduction Steps **Machine has dotnet-sdk-11.0.100-preview.7.26359.119 installed and has DOTNET_ROLL_FORWARD_ON_NO_CANDIDATE_FX=2 environment variable set.** 1.Open cmd and navigate to the application folder: `cd C:\Users\Test01\Desktop\GrandNode\GrandNode` 2. Launch application with command: `dotnet Grand.Web.dll` 3. Access http://localhost:5000/ from browser. ### Expected behavior Page load successfully in browser. ### Actual behavior Page load failed with “An error occurred while starting the application.”. ### Regression? Yes. 1). Windows 11 24H2 + dotnet-sdk-11.0.100-preview.6.26359.118: Pass 2). Windows 11 24H2 + dotnet-sdk-11.0.100-preview.7.26359.119: Fail 3). Windows 11 24H2 + dotnet-sdk-11.0.100-preview.7.26360.111: Fail ### Known Workarounds _No response_ ### Configuration **Application Name**: grandnode **OS**: Windows 11 24H2 **CPU**: X64 **.NET Build Number**: dotnet-sdk-11.0.100-preview.7.26360.111 **Application location and repro machine checking at**: https://devdiv.visualstudio.com/DevDiv/_workitems/edit/3030046 **Source Github link**: [appcompat-wx/grandnode at SourceFromAppCompatLab](https://github.com/appcompat-wx/grandnode/tree/SourceFromAppCompatLab) **dotnet --info** .NET SDK: Version: 11.0.100-preview.7.26360.111 Commit: 50dbab4de2 Workload version: 11.0.100-manifests.9a1cad8c MSBuild version: 18.10.0-1.26360.111+50dbab4de Runtime Environment: OS Name: Windows OS Version: 10.0.26100 OS Platform: Windows RID: win-x64 Base Path: C:\Program Files\dotnet\sdk\11.0.100-preview.7.26360.111\ .NET workloads installed: There are no installed workloads to display. Configured to use workload sets when installing new manifests. No workload sets are installed. Run "dotnet workload restore" to install a workload set. Host: Version: 11.0.0-preview.7.26360.111 Architecture: x64 Commit: 50dbab4de2 .NET SDKs installed: 11.0.100-preview.7.26360.111 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\sdk] .NET runtimes installed: Microsoft.AspNetCore.App 11.0.0-preview.7.26360.111 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.AspNetCore.App] Microsoft.NETCore.App 11.0.0-preview.7.26360.111 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App] Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App 11.0.0-preview.7.26360.111 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App] Other architectures found: None Environment variables: DOTNET_ROLL_FORWARD_ON_NO_CANDIDATE_FX [2] ### Other information @dotnet-actwx-bot @dotnet/compat

title: Virtualize unexpectedly jumps to the beginning/end of the list while scrolling after PR #65951 (.NET 11 Preview 4 regression)
author: BharatRamsf3693
body:
### Is there an existing issue for this? - [x] I have searched the existing issues ### Describe the bug After upgrading to .NET 11.0.0-preview.4, the Virtualize component exhibits unexpected scroll position changes. While scrolling through a virtualized list, the viewport automatically jumps: - When scrolling down, the list suddenly scrolls to the bottom. - When scrolling up, the list suddenly scrolls to the top (or another incorrect position). The issue is reproducible with both Items and ItemsProvider modes, but the behavior differs: **Using Items** - Issue occurs while scrolling both downward and upward. **Using ItemsProvider** - Issue occurs primarily while scrolling upward. Based on investigation, the regression appears to have been introduced by: PR #65951: "[Virtualization] Visible content does not shift when in-DOM items above the viewport change height" When the changes from that PR are reverted, the issue no longer occurs. The behavior started appearing from .NET 11 Preview 4. ### Expected Behavior Scrolling should remain stable. - Scrolling down should continue moving downward without jumping to the bottom. - Scrolling up should continue moving upward without jumping to the top. ### Steps To Reproduce **Case 1: Using Items** Create a new Blazor web application with server interactivity and use the following component: ``` @page "/" @rendermode InteractiveServer

In-Memory Virtualized List

Item @item.Index
@code { private List items = new(); protected override void OnInitialized() { items = Enumerable.Range(0, 1000) .Select(i => new DynamicItem { Index = i }) .ToList(); } public class DynamicItem { public int Index { get; set; } } } ``` **Reproduction Steps** 1. Run the application. 2. Scroll down through the virtualized list. 3. Observe unexpected jumps toward the bottom of the list. 4. Scroll upward. 5. Observe unexpected jumps toward the beginning of the list. **Case 2: Using ItemsProvider** ``` @page "/counter" @rendermode InteractiveServer

On-Demand Provider List

Item @item.Index
Loading...
@code { private async ValueTask> LoadItems(ItemsProviderRequest request) { // Total size of your dataset int totalItemsCount = 1000; // Simulate an asynchronous API or Database call (e.g., repository.GetPage(startIndex, numItems)) await Task.Delay(1000, request.CancellationToken); // Generate or fetch only the specific chunk requested var fetchedItems = Enumerable.Range(request.StartIndex, request.Count) .Select(i => new DynamicItem { Index = i }) .ToList(); // Return the specific slice and the total count so the scrollbar scales correctly return new ItemsProviderResult(fetchedItems, totalItemsCount); } public class DynamicItem { public int Index { get; set; } } } ``` **Reproduction Steps** 1. Run the application. 2. Scroll down to the bottom of the list. 3. Scroll upward. 4. Observe unexpected jumps toward the beginning of the list. ### Exceptions (if any) No exceptions are thrown. ### .NET Version 11.0.100-preview.4.26230.115 ### Anything else? **Affected Version** .NET 11.0.0-preview.4 and later (based on current investigation) **Last Known Working Version** .NET 11.0.0-preview.3 **Screenshots / Videos** I have attached a GIF demonstrating the issue below. [VirtualizeScrollingIssue.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29889919/VirtualizeScrollingIssue.zip)

title: API Proposal: CacheBoundary
author: dariatiurina

title: Using `TypedResults` and an API convention in the same project causes conflicts
author: cremor

title: SNI hostname and HTTP Host can drift, with no visibility
author: DeagleGross
body:
Kestrel selects the TLS profile from the ClientHello SNI (`SniOptionsSelector.OptionsCallback`, `src/Servers/Kestrel/Core/src/Internal/SniOptionsSelector.cs:179`), while HTTP-layer host authorization is performed independently against the `Host` header in `HostFilteringMiddleware` (`src/Middleware/HostFiltering/src/HostFilteringMiddleware.cs:82`). A client can legitimately pair any SNI with any `Host`, so the TLS policy applied to the connection and the host the request is routed to can diverge.

This is allowed by RFC 6066 §3 ("MAY use ... to guide its selection of ... security policy") and is required by scenarios like TLS-terminating proxies and connection reuse, so enforcing equality is not desirable. However, today there is no built-in way for an application to observe or enforce alignment when it wants to.

### Proposed fix (non-breaking)

Expose the SNI hostname used for TLS selection on a connection feature (e.g. extend `ITlsHandshakeFeature` or add `ISniHostnameFeature`) so middleware and auth handlers can compare it against `HttpContext.Request.Host` when their policy calls for it. Optionally add an opt-in Kestrel/HostFiltering option to reject requests whose `Host` does not fall under the matched SNI entry.

### Why

Apps that layer stricter TLS policy (mTLS, TLS 1.3-only, custom cipher policy) on specific SNI entries currently have no first-class way to detect when a request arrived under a different SNI than its `Host` claims. Making the SNI value observable lets applications add that check where their threat model requires it, without breaking legitimate SNI/Host mismatches.

Related #67721

title: SNI parsing accepts malformed server names
author: DeagleGross
body:
`SniOptionsSelector` does an exact-name lookup followed by an `EndsWith` scan of wildcard suffixes, with no validation that the SNI value is a well-formed DNS name.

- `src/Servers/Kestrel/Core/src/Internal/SniOptionsSelector.cs` stores `*.example.org` as the suffix `.example.org` and matches with `serverName.EndsWith(suffix, OrdinalIgnoreCase)` (constructor ~line 107, `GetOptions` ~lines 120–130).
- Inputs that are not syntactically valid DNS hostnames under RFC 6066 §3 / RFC 5890 (empty labels, leading/trailing dot, non-LDH characters, embedded nulls) are accepted and can match wildcard suffix entries they do not correspond to as real DNS names.

### Proposed fix

Validate `ClientHelloInfo.ServerName` before matching — reject or fall back to `_wildcardOptions` when the name is not a syntactically valid DNS hostname (e.g. `Uri.CheckHostName(name) == UriHostNameType.Dns` plus explicit rejection of empty labels and leading/trailing dots). Add unit tests in `SniOptionsSelectorTests` covering these malformed shapes.

### Why

The current matcher accepts inputs the TLS spec does not treat as legal hostnames, which makes SNI-based configuration harder to reason about and produces wildcard matches that don't correspond to any real DNS name.
